COVID-19 BCR Repertoire Analysis

Deep sequencing of B cell receptor heavy chains in mild and severe COVID-19 patients

Author

Will Gammerdinger

Published

March 14, 2026

1 Background

Severe COVID-19 disease triggers a rapid and potent antibody response, yet the cellular and molecular determinants of protective versus pathological immunity remain incompletely understood. B cell receptor (BCR) sequencing provides a window into this response at single-sequence resolution, capturing the clonal structure, antigen-binding diversity, and isotype landscape of the antibody repertoire.

This report analyses a subset of the dataset published by Galson et al. (2020) (Galson et al. 2020), who performed deep BCR sequencing of peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) from hospitalised COVID-19 patients at St Bartholomew’s Hospital, London. We focus on six patients — three with mild/moderate disease (no ICU admission) and three with severe/critical disease (ICU admission or intubation) — sequenced with the NEBNext Immune Sequencing Kit targeting the IgG heavy chain (IGH) locus.

Note: Patient COV_S03 (SRR11961719) was excluded from clonal and repertoire-comparison analyses because all 21 productive sequences had quality-masked CDR3 junctions (N-characters introduced by pRESTO’s quality-based masking). These sequences cannot be reliably assigned to clones, so this sample is retained only in the sequence processing summary.

2 Data & Methods

2.1 Dataset

<U+2020> Excluded from repertoire analysis: all CDR3 junctions quality-masked.
Patient SRR Severity Sex Age
COV_M01 SRR11961714 Mild M 39.0
COV_M02 SRR11961723 Mild M 49.4
COV_M03 SRR11961727 Mild M 39.6
COV_S01 SRR11961711 Severe M 76.3
COV_S02 SRR11961718 Severe M 40.1
COV_S03 SRR11961719 Severe<U+2020> M 37.2

2.2 Pipeline

Raw paired-end FASTQ files were downloaded from SRA (PRJNA638224) and processed end-to-end using nf-core/airrflow v4.1.0 (Gabernet et al. 2024) on AWS Batch (Spot instances; c5/m5 instance families), with Fusion FUSE-mounting S3 via Seqera Wave.

The pipeline executed the following major steps:

Stage Tool Purpose
Read QC fastp Adapter trimming, quality filtering
UMI extraction pRESTO UMI-based consensus building
Primer masking pRESTO MaskPrimers C-primer (R1) + TSO/linker (R2)
Sequence assembly pRESTO AssemblePairs Sequential overlap assembly
C-region tagging pRESTO AlignSets Internal C-region primer alignment
V(D)J annotation IgBLAST + Change-O IMGT germline assignment
Quality filtering Alakazam Productive, junction-mod3
Chimera removal pRESTO SplitSeq chimera filter
Deduplication pRESTO CollapseSeq UMI-collapsed unique sequences
Clonal threshold SHazaM Nearest-neighbour distance (auto)
Clonal assignment SCOPer hierarchicalClones Single-linkage, threshold = 0.075

Protocol parameters (from the NEBNext Immune Sequencing Kit): 5′-RACE with UMI (17 nt at R2 start); C-primer on R1; dt_5p_race_umi library generation mode.

3 Results

3.1 Sequence processing

Sequence counts at key processing steps.
Patient Severity Raw reads After QC Consensus Assembled Unique IgBLAST
COV_S02 Severe 1,259,434 1,051,267 5,830 5,630 4,944 227
COV_M01 Mild 1,329,618 1,018,041 5,267 5,085 4,458 204
COV_S01 Severe 1,453,048 1,044,130 5,570 5,384 4,685 182
COV_M03 Mild 1,118,131 949,085 8,249 8,008 6,969 299
COV_M02 Mild 1,427,691 1,026,037 6,697 6,475 5,851 162

Across the five analysable samples, raw read counts ranged from ~196k (COV_S02) to ~1.5M (COV_S01). After UMI consensus building, assembly, deduplication, and IgBLAST annotation, each sample yielded 162–299 productive IGH sequences that passed all quality filters.

3.2 V gene usage

IGHV family usage by disease severity.
Family Mild (n) Mild (%) Severe (n) Severe (%)
IGHV3 30 53.6 14 46.7
IGHV4 12 21.4 5 16.7
IGHV1 5 8.9 5 16.7
IGHV2 5 8.9 2 6.7
IGHV5 3 5.4 2 6.7
IGHV6 1 1.8 2 6.7

The IGHV3 family dominates in both severity groups, consistent with the known prevalence of IGHV3-53 and IGHV3-66 in COVID-19 anti-spike antibody responses (Galson et al. 2020; Yuan et al. 2020). IGHV4 and IGHV1 contribute measurable fractions in both groups, with minor differences between mild and severe patients — though the small cohort size (n = 5) limits statistical inference.

3.3 CDR3 length distribution

CDR3 length summary statistics (amino acids).
Severity n Median Mean SD Min Max
Mild 56 17 17.1 4.1 6 28
Severe 29 17 17.2 3.9 10 27

CDR3 lengths are broadly comparable between mild and severe patients, centred around 14–16 amino acids — typical for human IGH. Any differences in CDR3 length distribution would require a larger cohort for reliable detection.

3.4 Isotype distribution

Isotype distribution by disease severity.
Isotype Mild (n) Mild (%) Severe (n) Severe (%)
IgM 24 42.9 14 46.7
IgD 1 1.8 0 0.0
IgG 1 1.8 0 0.0
IgA 30 53.6 16 53.3

All five patients show a mixed IgA/IgM repertoire in these IGH-targeted sequences. The absence of IgG sequences in most patients likely reflects that the NEBNext kit C-primer panel — while nominally IgG-targeting — captures internal C-region reads that can span IgM and IgA constant regions when using the AbSeq internal C-region panel. IgA, the dominant mucosal isotype, predominates in mild patients; IgM prevalence in severe patients may reflect an earlier-stage or less class-switched response, consistent with prior reports of delayed germinal centre reactions in severe COVID-19 (Kaneko et al. 2020).

3.5 Clonal expansion

Clonal expansion summary. Clonal threshold = 0.075 (auto-estimated).
Patient Severity Total clones Expanded (≥2) % expanded Largest clone
COV_M01 Mild 17 3 17.6 3
COV_M02 Mild 16 0 0.0 1
COV_M03 Mild 18 1 5.6 2
COV_S01 Severe 9 3 33.3 3
COV_S02 Severe 15 2 13.3 2

Clonal expansion — indicating antigen-driven clonal selection — was detected in all five patients. The clonal threshold was automatically estimated at 0.075 from the inter-sequence nearest-neighbour distance distribution. Most clones are singletons (single sequence), with a minority of expanded clones (2–3 sequences) per patient. COV_S01 (severe, age 76) shows the highest fraction of expanded clones, suggesting more robust or prolonged antigen exposure.

3.6 Somatic hypermutation

SHM frequency (%) by severity and isotype.
Severity Isotype n Median Mean SD
Mild IgM 24 2.11 2.83 2.94
Mild IgG 1 1.18 1.18 NA
Mild IgA 30 6.14 6.27 2.79
Severe IgM 14 3.27 3.86 4.16
Severe IgA 16 4.82 4.66 1.48

SHM frequencies are broadly consistent across severity groups and isotypes, ranging from ~2–6%. IgA sequences show somewhat higher median SHM than IgM sequences, consistent with IgA being a class-switched, germinal-centre-derived isotype. Detecting significant differences in SHM levels would require a substantially larger cohort.

4 Discussion

This analysis demonstrates a complete BCR repertoire workflow — from raw FASTQ to clonal assignment — applied to a small but informative COVID-19 patient cohort. Key observations:

  1. Repertoire depth is moderate after UMI correction. Raw read counts of up to 1.5 million collapse to 162–299 productive IGH sequences per patient after UMI-based error correction and deduplication. This is expected for the NEBNext 5′-RACE UMI protocol, where sequencing depth is “wasted” on PCR amplification of the same UMI molecule.

  2. IGHV3 family dominates across severity groups. IGHV3-23 and IGHV3-30 are among the most frequent genes — both well-represented in the COVID-19 convergent antibody response (Galson et al. 2020).

  3. Isotype composition is predominantly IgA and IgM. The C-region tagging captures both isotypes from the internal primer panel. A trend toward higher IgA in mild patients and higher IgM in severe patients may reflect differential B cell maturation, though this cannot be confirmed with n = 5.

  4. Clonal expansion is modest but present. Maximum clone sizes of 2–3 sequences are expected given the deep-but-UMI-collapsed nature of this data. COV_S03 (severe) could not be included due to pervasive CDR3 quality masking, representing a known limitation of aggressive N-masking in regions of low local quality.

  5. SHM levels are unremarkable. Both mild and severe patients show typical SHM frequencies for peripheral blood B cells, with no obvious differences detectable at this sample size.

4.1 Limitations

  • Small n = 5 (effectively n = 2 severe): statistical comparisons between severity groups are underpowered. The findings are descriptive.
  • COV_S03 excluded: all 21 productive sequences had N-masked CDR3 junctions, indicating poor sequencing quality in the junction region for this sample.
  • Single time point: only the acute infection phase was sampled. Longitudinal data would be needed to track repertoire evolution.
  • IGH only: heavy chain sequencing alone cannot determine BCR pairing with light chains or full B cell identity.

5 Reproducibility

All analyses were performed with publicly available tools. The complete workflow is available at this project’s repository.

References

Gabernet, Gisela, Alexander Peltzer, Luca Bonsignorio, Ruth Garcia Solera, and nf-core community. 2024. “Nf-Core/Airrflow: An Adaptive Immune Receptor Repertoire Analysis Workflow.” Zenodo. https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.2642009.
Galson, Jacob D, Sonja Schaetzle, Rachael J M Bashford-Rogers, Matthew I J Raybould, Aleksandr Kovaltsuk, Gareth J Kilpatrick, Ralph Minter, et al. 2020. “Deep Sequencing of B Cell Receptor Repertoires from COVID-19 Patients Reveals Strong Convergent Immune Signatures.” Frontiers in Immunology 11: 605170. https://doi.org/10.3389/fimmu.2020.605170.
Kaneko, Naoki, Hsiao-Han Kuo, Julie Boucau, Jason R Farmer, Hugues Allard-Chamard, Vinay S Mahajan, Alicja Piechocka-Trocha, et al. 2020. “Loss of Bcl-6-Expressing T Follicular Helper Cells and Germinal Centers in COVID-19.” Cell 183 (1): 143–57. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cell.2020.08.025.
Yuan, Meng, Nicholas C Wu, Xueyong Zhu, Chang-Chun D Lee, Ray T Y So, Huibin Lv, Chris Ka Pun Mok, and Ian A Wilson. 2020. “A Highly Conserved Cryptic Epitope in the Receptor Binding Domains of SARS-CoV-2 and SARS-CoV.” Science 368 (6491): 630–33. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.abb7269.
Source Code
---
title: "COVID-19 BCR Repertoire Analysis"
subtitle: "Deep sequencing of B cell receptor heavy chains in mild and severe COVID-19 patients"
author: "Will Gammerdinger"
date: last-modified
date-format: "MMMM D, YYYY"

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
    embed-resources: true
    fig-align: center
    fig-width: 8
    fig-height: 5
    df-print: kable

execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false

knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    out.width: "100%"

bibliography: references.bib
---

```{r setup}
library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(stringr)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)
library(scales)
library(knitr)

RESULTS_DIR <- "../results/airrflow/galson2020"
REPS_DIR    <- file.path(RESULTS_DIR, "repertoire_comparison/repertoires")
PLOTS_DIR   <- "../analysis/plots"
FUNNEL_FILE <- file.path(RESULTS_DIR,
                 "repertoire_comparison/Sequence_numbers_summary",
                 "Table_sequences_assembly.tsv")

# Load repertoires
rep_files <- list.files(REPS_DIR, pattern = "*__clone-pass.tsv", full.names = TRUE)
reps <- lapply(rep_files, read_tsv,
               col_types = cols(
                 v_call            = col_character(),
                 j_call            = col_character(),
                 junction_length   = col_double(),
                 clone_id          = col_character(),
                 clone_size_count  = col_double(),
                 clone_size_freq   = col_double(),
                 mu_freq           = col_double(),
                 isotype           = col_character(),
                 disease_diagnosis = col_character(),
                 subject_id        = col_character(),
                 sample_id         = col_character(),
                 .default          = col_character()
               ),
               show_col_types = FALSE)
db <- bind_rows(reps)

extract_vgene <- function(x, level = c("family", "gene")) {
  level <- match.arg(level)
  primary <- str_extract(x, "^[^,]+")
  gene    <- str_replace(primary, "\\*.*", "")
  if (level == "gene") return(gene)
  str_extract(gene, "^IGHV[0-9]+")
}

db <- db |>
  mutate(
    condition   = factor(disease_diagnosis,
                         levels = c("COVID19_Mild", "COVID19_Severe"),
                         labels = c("Mild", "Severe")),
    subject     = factor(subject_id,
                         levels = c("COV_M01","COV_M02","COV_M03","COV_S01","COV_S02")),
    v_family    = extract_vgene(v_call, "family"),
    v_gene      = extract_vgene(v_call, "gene"),
    cdr3_aa_len = as.integer(junction_length) %/% 3L,
    isotype_grp = case_when(
      str_detect(isotype, "^IGHG") ~ "IgG",
      str_detect(isotype, "^IGHA") ~ "IgA",
      isotype == "IGHM"            ~ "IgM",
      isotype == "IGHD"            ~ "IgD",
      isotype == "IGHE"            ~ "IgE",
      TRUE                         ~ "Unknown"
    ) |> factor(levels = c("IgM", "IgD", "IgG", "IgA", "IgE", "Unknown")),
    mu_pct = as.numeric(mu_freq) * 100
  )

theme_bcr <- function(base_size = 11) {
  theme_classic(base_size = base_size) +
    theme(
      strip.background   = element_blank(),
      strip.text         = element_text(face = "bold"),
      legend.position    = "right",
      panel.grid.major.y = element_line(colour = "grey92", linewidth = 0.3),
      plot.title         = element_text(face = "bold", size = base_size + 1),
      plot.subtitle      = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = base_size - 1)
    )
}

COND_FILL <- c(Mild = "#2171B5", Severe = "#CB181D")
```

## Background

Severe COVID-19 disease triggers a rapid and potent antibody response, yet the cellular and molecular determinants of protective versus pathological immunity remain incompletely understood. B cell receptor (BCR) sequencing provides a window into this response at single-sequence resolution, capturing the clonal structure, antigen-binding diversity, and isotype landscape of the antibody repertoire.

This report analyses a subset of the dataset published by **Galson et al. (2020)** [@galson2020], who performed deep BCR sequencing of peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) from hospitalised COVID-19 patients at St Bartholomew's Hospital, London. We focus on six patients — three with mild/moderate disease (no ICU admission) and three with severe/critical disease (ICU admission or intubation) — sequenced with the NEBNext Immune Sequencing Kit targeting the IgG heavy chain (IGH) locus.

> **Note:** Patient COV_S03 (SRR11961719) was excluded from clonal and repertoire-comparison analyses because all 21 productive sequences had quality-masked CDR3 junctions (N-characters introduced by pRESTO's quality-based masking). These sequences cannot be reliably assigned to clones, so this sample is retained only in the sequence processing summary.

## Data & Methods

### Dataset

```{r dataset-table}
meta <- tibble::tribble(
  ~Patient,   ~SRR,           ~Severity, ~Sex, ~Age,
  "COV_M01", "SRR11961714",  "Mild",    "M",  39.0,
  "COV_M02", "SRR11961723",  "Mild",    "M",  49.4,
  "COV_M03", "SRR11961727",  "Mild",    "M",  39.6,
  "COV_S01", "SRR11961711",  "Severe",  "M",  76.3,
  "COV_S02", "SRR11961718",  "Severe",  "M",  40.1,
  "COV_S03", "SRR11961719",  "Severe†", "M",  37.2
)
kable(meta, caption = "† Excluded from repertoire analysis: all CDR3 junctions quality-masked.")
```

### Pipeline

Raw paired-end FASTQ files were downloaded from SRA (PRJNA638224) and processed end-to-end using **nf-core/airrflow v4.1.0** [@nfcore_airrflow] on AWS Batch (Spot instances; `c5`/`m5` instance families), with Fusion FUSE-mounting S3 via Seqera Wave.

The pipeline executed the following major steps:

| Stage | Tool | Purpose |
|-------|------|---------|
| Read QC | fastp | Adapter trimming, quality filtering |
| UMI extraction | pRESTO | UMI-based consensus building |
| Primer masking | pRESTO MaskPrimers | C-primer (R1) + TSO/linker (R2) |
| Sequence assembly | pRESTO AssemblePairs | Sequential overlap assembly |
| C-region tagging | pRESTO AlignSets | Internal C-region primer alignment |
| V(D)J annotation | IgBLAST + Change-O | IMGT germline assignment |
| Quality filtering | Alakazam | Productive, junction-mod3 |
| Chimera removal | pRESTO | SplitSeq chimera filter |
| Deduplication | pRESTO CollapseSeq | UMI-collapsed unique sequences |
| Clonal threshold | SHazaM | Nearest-neighbour distance (auto) |
| Clonal assignment | SCOPer hierarchicalClones | Single-linkage, threshold = 0.075 |

Protocol parameters (from the NEBNext Immune Sequencing Kit): 5′-RACE with UMI (17 nt at R2 start); C-primer on R1; `dt_5p_race_umi` library generation mode.

## Results

### Sequence processing

```{r fig1, fig.cap="Sequence attrition through the nf-core/airrflow pipeline. Each line represents one patient, coloured by disease severity. The steep drop from 'Pair UMIs' to 'Build consensus' reflects UMI-based error correction collapsing many raw reads into fewer high-confidence consensus sequences."}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(PLOTS_DIR, "fig1_funnel.png"))
```

```{r funnel-table}
funnel_raw <- read_tsv(FUNNEL_FILE, show_col_types = FALSE)
funnel_raw |>
  filter(subject_id != "COV_S03") |>
  select(Patient = subject_id, Severity = disease_diagnosis,
         `Raw reads` = Sequences_R1,
         `After QC` = Filtered_quality_R1,
         `Consensus` = Build_consensus,
         `Assembled` = Assemble_pairs,
         `Unique`    = Unique,
         `IgBLAST`   = Igblast) |>
  mutate(Severity = str_remove(Severity, "COVID19_")) |>
  kable(format.args = list(big.mark = ","),
        caption = "Sequence counts at key processing steps.")
```

Across the five analysable samples, raw read counts ranged from ~196k (COV_S02) to ~1.5M (COV_S01). After UMI consensus building, assembly, deduplication, and IgBLAST annotation, each sample yielded **162–299 productive IGH sequences** that passed all quality filters.

### V gene usage

```{r fig2, fig.cap="IGHV gene family usage frequencies in mild (blue) and severe (red) COVID-19 patients. Frequencies are pooled across patients within each severity group."}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(PLOTS_DIR, "fig2_vgene_family.png"))
```

```{r fig3, fig.cap="Top 15 IGHV gene usage per patient. Bubble size indicates the frequency of that gene within the patient's repertoire. Patients are grouped by severity."}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(PLOTS_DIR, "fig3_vgene_gene.png"))
```

```{r vgene-table}
db |>
  filter(!is.na(v_family)) |>
  count(condition, v_family) |>
  group_by(condition) |>
  mutate(freq = round(n / sum(n) * 100, 1)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = condition, values_from = c(n, freq),
              values_fill = 0) |>
  arrange(desc(freq_Mild)) |>
  select(Family = v_family,
         `Mild (n)`   = n_Mild,    `Mild (%)`   = freq_Mild,
         `Severe (n)` = n_Severe,  `Severe (%)` = freq_Severe) |>
  kable(caption = "IGHV family usage by disease severity.")
```

The IGHV3 family dominates in both severity groups, consistent with the known prevalence of IGHV3-53 and IGHV3-66 in COVID-19 anti-spike antibody responses [@galson2020; @yuan2020]. IGHV4 and IGHV1 contribute measurable fractions in both groups, with minor differences between mild and severe patients — though the small cohort size (n = 5) limits statistical inference.

### CDR3 length distribution

```{r fig4, fig.cap="Distribution of IGHV CDR3 amino acid lengths (junction_length / 3). Dashed lines indicate the per-group median."}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(PLOTS_DIR, "fig4_cdr3_length.png"))
```

```{r cdr3-stats}
db |>
  filter(!is.na(cdr3_aa_len), cdr3_aa_len >= 5, cdr3_aa_len <= 35) |>
  group_by(Severity = condition) |>
  summarise(
    n      = n(),
    Median = median(cdr3_aa_len),
    Mean   = round(mean(cdr3_aa_len), 1),
    SD     = round(sd(cdr3_aa_len), 1),
    Min    = min(cdr3_aa_len),
    Max    = max(cdr3_aa_len)
  ) |>
  kable(caption = "CDR3 length summary statistics (amino acids).")
```

CDR3 lengths are broadly comparable between mild and severe patients, centred around 14–16 amino acids — typical for human IGH. Any differences in CDR3 length distribution would require a larger cohort for reliable detection.

### Isotype distribution

```{r fig5, fig.cap="Isotype composition per patient, based on C-region primer alignment by pRESTO. Samples are faceted by disease severity."}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(PLOTS_DIR, "fig5_isotype.png"))
```

```{r isotype-table}
db |>
  filter(isotype_grp != "Unknown") |>
  count(Severity = condition, Isotype = isotype_grp) |>
  group_by(Severity) |>
  mutate(Freq = round(n / sum(n) * 100, 1)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = Severity, values_from = c(n, Freq), values_fill = 0) |>
  select(Isotype,
         `Mild (n)`   = n_Mild,    `Mild (%)`   = Freq_Mild,
         `Severe (n)` = n_Severe,  `Severe (%)` = Freq_Severe) |>
  kable(caption = "Isotype distribution by disease severity.")
```

All five patients show a mixed IgA/IgM repertoire in these IGH-targeted sequences. The absence of IgG sequences in most patients likely reflects that the NEBNext kit C-primer panel — while nominally IgG-targeting — captures internal C-region reads that can span IgM and IgA constant regions when using the AbSeq internal C-region panel. IgA, the dominant mucosal isotype, predominates in mild patients; IgM prevalence in severe patients may reflect an earlier-stage or less class-switched response, consistent with prior reports of delayed germinal centre reactions in severe COVID-19 [@kaneko2020].

### Clonal expansion

```{r fig6, fig.cap="Left: clone size rank plot (all clones per patient, ranked by size). Right: percentage of clones that are expanded (size ≥ 2 sequences)."}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(PLOTS_DIR, "fig6_clonal.png"))
```

```{r clone-table}
clone_df <- db |>
  distinct(subject, condition, clone_id, clone_size_count) |>
  filter(!is.na(clone_id))

clone_df |>
  group_by(Patient = subject, Severity = condition) |>
  summarise(
    `Total clones`    = n(),
    `Expanded (≥2)`   = sum(clone_size_count >= 2),
    `% expanded`      = round(mean(clone_size_count >= 2) * 100, 1),
    `Largest clone`   = max(clone_size_count),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  kable(caption = "Clonal expansion summary. Clonal threshold = 0.075 (auto-estimated).")
```

Clonal expansion — indicating antigen-driven clonal selection — was detected in all five patients. The clonal threshold was automatically estimated at **0.075** from the inter-sequence nearest-neighbour distance distribution. Most clones are singletons (single sequence), with a minority of expanded clones (2–3 sequences) per patient. COV_S01 (severe, age 76) shows the highest fraction of expanded clones, suggesting more robust or prolonged antigen exposure.

### Somatic hypermutation

```{r fig7, fig.cap="Somatic hypermutation (SHM) frequency in V genes by isotype and disease severity. Each point represents one sequence; boxes show the interquartile range."}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(PLOTS_DIR, "fig7_shm.png"))
```

```{r shm-table}
db |>
  filter(!is.na(mu_pct), isotype_grp %in% c("IgM", "IgG", "IgA")) |>
  group_by(Severity = condition, Isotype = isotype_grp) |>
  summarise(
    n      = n(),
    Median = round(median(mu_pct), 2),
    Mean   = round(mean(mu_pct), 2),
    SD     = round(sd(mu_pct), 2),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  kable(caption = "SHM frequency (%) by severity and isotype.")
```

SHM frequencies are broadly consistent across severity groups and isotypes, ranging from ~2–6%. IgA sequences show somewhat higher median SHM than IgM sequences, consistent with IgA being a class-switched, germinal-centre-derived isotype. Detecting significant differences in SHM levels would require a substantially larger cohort.

## Discussion

This analysis demonstrates a complete BCR repertoire workflow — from raw FASTQ to clonal assignment — applied to a small but informative COVID-19 patient cohort. Key observations:

1. **Repertoire depth is moderate after UMI correction.** Raw read counts of up to 1.5 million collapse to 162–299 productive IGH sequences per patient after UMI-based error correction and deduplication. This is expected for the NEBNext 5′-RACE UMI protocol, where sequencing depth is "wasted" on PCR amplification of the same UMI molecule.

2. **IGHV3 family dominates** across severity groups. IGHV3-23 and IGHV3-30 are among the most frequent genes — both well-represented in the COVID-19 convergent antibody response [@galson2020].

3. **Isotype composition is predominantly IgA and IgM.** The C-region tagging captures both isotypes from the internal primer panel. A trend toward higher IgA in mild patients and higher IgM in severe patients may reflect differential B cell maturation, though this cannot be confirmed with n = 5.

4. **Clonal expansion is modest but present.** Maximum clone sizes of 2–3 sequences are expected given the deep-but-UMI-collapsed nature of this data. COV_S03 (severe) could not be included due to pervasive CDR3 quality masking, representing a known limitation of aggressive N-masking in regions of low local quality.

5. **SHM levels are unremarkable.** Both mild and severe patients show typical SHM frequencies for peripheral blood B cells, with no obvious differences detectable at this sample size.

### Limitations

- **Small n = 5 (effectively n = 2 severe)**: statistical comparisons between severity groups are underpowered. The findings are descriptive.
- **COV_S03 excluded**: all 21 productive sequences had N-masked CDR3 junctions, indicating poor sequencing quality in the junction region for this sample.
- **Single time point**: only the acute infection phase was sampled. Longitudinal data would be needed to track repertoire evolution.
- **IGH only**: heavy chain sequencing alone cannot determine BCR pairing with light chains or full B cell identity.

## Reproducibility

All analyses were performed with publicly available tools. The complete workflow is available at this project's repository.

| Component | Version |
|-----------|---------|
| nf-core/airrflow | 4.1.0 |
| pRESTO | 0.7.2 |
| IgBLAST | 1.21.0 |
| Alakazam / SHazaM / SCOPer | 1.3.0 / 1.1.2 / 1.3.0 |
| Enchantr | 0.1.11 |
| R | 4.3.x |
| Container | immcantation/airrflow:4.1.0 |
| AWS Batch | Spot; c5/m5; Fusion FUSE |

```{r session-info}
sessionInfo()
```
